threats.title: update_market Uses Current Block Number for All Historical Epoch Time Calculations, Enabling Retroactive Reward Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: In LendingLedger.update_market, the epochTime variable is computed using block.number (current block) inside the while loop rather than the iterator variable i (historical block being processed). This means gauge_relative_weight_write is called with the CURRENT estimated timestamp for ALL historical epoch iterations. An attacker can manipulate current gauge weights (via vote_for_gauge_weights) and then trigger update_market on a stale market to retroactively apply favorable gauge weights to all un-updated historical epochs, claiming disproportionate CANTO rewards.
threats.investigation_rationale: The epochTime formula should use the loop iterator i instead of block.number. All historical epochs receive the same gauge weight as the current period, enabling retroactive weight manipulation for reward theft.

proof_of_signal.pattern: epochTime uses block.number instead of loop variable i; epochTime = referenceBlockTime + ((block.number - referenceBlockNumber) * averageBlockTime) / 1000

preconditions: Market has not had update_market called for multiple epochs (stale lastRewardBlock)
Market has positive lendingMarketTotalBalance
Attacker has veCANTO voting power to change gauge weights
cantoPerBlock is nonzero for the relevant epochs

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: epochTime computed with block.number instead of i in while loop iterating from lastRewardBlock to block.number
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: All iterations of the reward accumulation loop use the same current-block epochTime, causing gauge_relative_weight_write to return current weights for all historical periods
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No per-iteration time calculation; should use i instead of block.number

analysis_questions: How many epochs can a market remain stale before someone calls update_market?
What is the maximum reward inflation factor achievable by manipulating current weights?
Does gauge_relative_weight_write return the same value for all loop iterations within one tx?
validation_requirements: Calculate the difference in rewards between using historical vs current gauge weights
Verify that gauge_relative_weight_write returns current-period weight for future epochTime values
Test end-to-end attack: vote, wait for staleness, update_market, claim

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker votes to give a low-weight market 90% gauge weight. Calls update_market on a market stale for 10 epochs. All 10 epochs are credited at 90% weight instead of historical 10%. Attacker's proportional share yields 9x expected rewards.
prerequisites: veCANTO voting power
A market with stale lastRewardBlock
Deposited position in the target market

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Massive over-distribution of CANTO rewards to attacker's chosen market. Could drain entire LendingLedger CANTO balance.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All legitimate users across all markets receive fewer rewards
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Difficult — rewards are sent immediately on claim; no clawback mechanism

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying epochTime accuracy per iteration in update_market

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Gas cost of update_market over many epochs
technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Sufficient veCANTO to shift gauge weights significantly
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable on-chain via gauge weight changes before market updates

threats.title: GaugeController.remove_gauge Always Reverts for Gauges with Active Weight Due to Type-0 Underflow
threats.hypothesis_description: In remove_gauge, gauge_types_[_gauge] is set to 0 BEFORE calling _remove_gauge_weight. Inside _remove_gauge_weight, gauge_type = gauge_types_[_gauge] - 1 evaluates to -1 (int128). _get_sum(-1) returns 0 because time_sum[-1] is uninitialized. The subsequent calculation new_sum = 0 - old_weight_bias causes a uint256 underflow revert in Solidity 0.8 whenever old_weight_bias > 0. This makes remove_gauge permanently non-functional for any gauge that has active votes.
threats.investigation_rationale: The ordering of state mutation (zeroing gauge_types_ before calling _remove_gauge_weight) is demonstrably incorrect. The function is broken for its primary use case.

proof_of_signal.pattern: gauge_types_[_gauge] = 0 executed before _remove_gauge_weight(_gauge) which reads gauge_types_[_gauge] - 1

preconditions: Target gauge has any positive weight (votes allocated to it)
Governance calls remove_gauge without first calling remove_gauge_weight

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: State (gauge_types_) zeroed before internal function reads it; _remove_gauge_weight reads gauge_types_[_gauge]-1 = -1; _get_sum(-1) returns 0; new_sum = 0 - old_weight_bias underflows
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: remove_gauge is non-functional for active gauges — governance cannot remove malicious or deprecated gauges
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: gauge_types_ should be zeroed AFTER _remove_gauge_weight, or type should be passed as parameter

analysis_questions: Can governance work around this by calling remove_gauge_weight before remove_gauge?
Even with the workaround, does _remove_gauge_weight in remove_gauge corrupt storage by writing to type -1?
Are there any gauges in the system that cannot be removed at all?
validation_requirements: Attempt remove_gauge on a gauge with positive votes; confirm revert
Test workaround sequence: remove_gauge_weight then remove_gauge
Check storage writes to points_sum[-1] for corruption

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Governance deploys a gauge that turns malicious. Governance attempts to remove it via remove_gauge but the transaction always reverts due to underflow. The malicious gauge continues receiving emissions indefinitely.
prerequisites: Gauge has any positive vote weight

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Inability to remove compromised gauges means continued misdirection of CANTO emissions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All veCANTO holders and liquidity providers affected by malicious gauge emissions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Partial workaround exists via separate remove_gauge_weight call, but governance may not know this

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for remove_gauge on a gauge with active votes

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Easily detectable — governance tx reverts

threats.title: Users' Vote Power Permanently Locked When Gauges Are Removed
threats.hypothesis_description: After a gauge is removed (gauge_types_ set to 0), users who had votes allocated to that gauge cannot reclaim their vote_user_power. vote_for_gauge_weights allows _user_weight=0 on removed gauges (first require passes), but then gauge_type = gauge_types_[_gauge_addr] - 1 = -1, and require(gauge_type >= 0) fails, causing a revert. The user's vote_user_power permanently includes the locked bps, reducing their effective voting capacity forever.
threats.investigation_rationale: The two require statements in vote_for_gauge_weights contradict each other: the first allows weight-0 votes on non-gauges, but the second blocks execution for non-gauges. Users can never recover allocated power from removed gauges.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(_user_weight == 0 || gauge_types_[_gauge_addr] != 0) passes for weight=0 on removed gauge, but gauge_type = 0 - 1 = -1 fails require(gauge_type >= 0)

preconditions: User has previously voted for a gauge with nonzero weight
Governance removes that gauge (gauge_types_ set to 0)
User attempts to vote 0 on the removed gauge to reclaim power

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Contradictory require statements: first allows 0-weight votes on removed gauges, second blocks execution due to gauge_type = -1
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: vote_user_power[user] permanently includes locked bps; user voting capacity permanently degraded
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No mechanism to reclaim vote power from removed gauges; no governance function to reset user votes

analysis_questions: How many users could be affected by a single gauge removal?
Is there any other path to reclaim vote_user_power?
Can governance deploy a new gauge at the same address to unblock vote reclamation?
validation_requirements: Vote for gauge, remove gauge, attempt to vote 0 — confirm revert at line 250
Check vote_user_power remains inflated after gauge removal
Verify no alternative recovery mechanism exists

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User allocates 5000 bps to gauge A. Governance removes gauge A. User's effective voting capacity is reduced to 5000 bps forever. After multiple gauge removals, users may lose all voting ability.
prerequisites: User has voted for a gauge that gets removed

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Users lose proportional control over CANTO emissions direction; emissions become inefficiently allocated
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users who voted for any removed gauge permanently lose voting capacity
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None — no function to reset vote_user_power; no governance override

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for voting 0 on a removed gauge

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Users discover when trying to reallocate votes

threats.title: accCantoPerShare Lacks 1e18 Precision Scaling Leading to Reward Truncation for Normal Market Conditions
threats.hypothesis_description: In update_market, the accCantoPerShare accumulation formula is: (blockDelta * cantoPerBlock[epoch] * gaugeRelativeWeight) / marketSupply. There is no intermediate 1e18 scaling factor before the division. Compare with secRewardsPerShare which scales by 1e36. When cantoPerBlock * gaugeRelativeWeight is small relative to marketSupply (e.g., low emission rate, small gauge weight, large market supply), the division truncates to zero, permanently losing rewards for those blocks. This is unlike standard MasterChef implementations that multiply by 1e12 before dividing.
threats.investigation_rationale: The formula relies on cantoPerBlock * gaugeRelativeWeight (up to ~1e36) being much larger than marketSupply. For markets with large supply and low gauge weight, precision loss accumulates significantly.

proof_of_signal.pattern: (blockDelta * cantoPerBlock[epoch] * gaugeController.gauge_relative_weight_write(...)) / marketSupply — no intermediate precision scaling before division

preconditions: Market has high total supply (e.g., > 1e24)
cantoPerBlock * gaugeRelativeWeight < marketSupply for single-block delta
blockDelta = 1 (per-block granularity iterations)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Integer division without prior upscaling; numerator can be smaller than denominator
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Rewards silently lost to truncation; no excess tracking or correction mechanism
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing 1e18 multiplication before division (standard MasterChef pattern)

analysis_questions: What is the expected marketSupply range for whitelisted markets?
For realistic cantoPerBlock values, at what marketSupply does truncation to 0 occur?
Does secRewardsPerShare (with 1e36 scaling) prove the developers knew precision was needed but missed it for accCantoPerShare?
validation_requirements: Calculate reward loss for realistic parameters (1e18 cantoPerBlock, 1e15 relativeWeight, 1e24 supply)
Compare expected vs actual reward distributions over extended periods
Quantify cumulative truncation loss

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: With cantoPerBlock = 1e16, relativeWeight = 1e14, marketSupply = 1e22: numerator per block = 1e16 * 1e14 = 1e30 / 1e22 = 1e8. With claim dividing by 1e18: user with 1e22 tokens gets 1e22 * 1e8 / 1e18 = 1e12 per block = negligible. But if cantoPerBlock = 1e14 and marketSupply = 1e24: numerator = 1e14 * 1e18 / 1e24 = 1e8, fine. It becomes 0 when numerator < marketSupply.
prerequisites: Large market supply relative to emission rate and gauge weight

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Partial to complete loss of CANTO rewards for depositors in high-TVL, low-weight markets
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All depositors in affected market
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Lost rewards cannot be recovered; CANTO stays in LendingLedger contract

threats.test_coverage_gap: No precision testing for edge-case market supply / emission ratios

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Difficult to detect; rewards appear lower than expected

threats.title: LendingLedger.sync_ledger Lacks Access Control Allowing Any Whitelisted Market to Manipulate Arbitrary User Balances
threats.hypothesis_description: sync_ledger has no access control beyond the implicit whitelist check in update_market. Any contract address that is directly whitelisted (markets without gauges) can call sync_ledger with arbitrary _lender addresses and _delta values. If ANY whitelisted market contract is compromised, buggy, or malicious, it can inflate or deflate any user's balance and reward debt, stealing CANTO rewards from LendingLedger. The trust boundary relies entirely on ALL whitelisted markets being honest for ALL users.
threats.investigation_rationale: A single compromised whitelisted market can manipulate all users' reward accounting across that market, without any per-user authorization check in sync_ledger.
deployment_assumptions: All whitelisted market contracts are honest and uncompromised
threats.config_evidence: LendingLedger.sol whiteListLendingMarket grants unrestricted sync_ledger access to market addresses. No deployment script shows which markets will be whitelisted.

proof_of_signal.pattern: sync_ledger takes arbitrary _lender and _delta from msg.sender; only check is market whitelist; no per-user authorization

preconditions: A whitelisted market contract is compromised or malicious
The compromised market calls sync_ledger with inflated _delta for an attacker address

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No verification that _lender actually deposited in the calling market; msg.sender = market is sufficient to modify any user's balance
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Compromised market can inflate attacker's balance to claim all CANTO rewards
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No per-user deposit verification; no cross-check with actual market balances

analysis_questions: What types of contracts will be whitelisted as markets?
Do those contracts have upgrade mechanisms that could be exploited?
Is there a governance process to verify market contract security before whitelisting?
validation_requirements: Deploy a malicious market contract, get it whitelisted, call sync_ledger with inflated delta
Verify attacker can claim CANTO rewards for the fake balance
Quantify maximum extractable value

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Compromised whitelisted market calls sync_ledger(attacker, 1e30). Attacker's balance inflated to 1e30. Attacker calls claim and receives all accumulated CANTO rewards for that market.
prerequisites: A whitelisted market contract is compromised

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete drain of LendingLedger CANTO balance
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users across all markets lose pending rewards
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None after CANTO is drained

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for malicious market calling sync_ledger with arbitrary parameters

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Control of a whitelisted market contract
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring sync_ledger events for abnormal deltas

threats.title: Missing delegate/undelegate Functions Render VotingEscrow Delegation Architecture Dead Code
threats.hypothesis_description: The VotingEscrow contract architecturally supports delegation through LockedBalance.delegatee and LockedBalance.delegated fields, LockAction.DELEGATE and UNDELEGATE enum values, and an explicit delegation code path in increaseAmount. However, no external delegate() or undelegate() functions are implemented. Users can never change their delegatee from self, meaning the entire delegation subsystem is dead code. The QUIT lock action also has no corresponding function, leaving users with no early exit mechanism other than the global unlockOverride.
threats.investigation_rationale: Multiple code paths handle delegation logic that can never execute. The missing functions represent a significant feature gap — users expected to delegate voting power have no mechanism to do so, and no individual early exit exists.

proof_of_signal.pattern: LockAction enum includes DELEGATE, UNDELEGATE, QUIT with no corresponding external functions; LockedBalance has delegatee/delegated fields; increaseAmount has delegation branch at lines 271-281

preconditions: User wants to delegate voting power or exit lock early

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: VotingEscrow.sol lines 63-69 (enum) and lines 271-281 (delegation branch in increaseAmount)
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Dead code: delegation branch in increaseAmount is unreachable because delegatee always equals msg.sender; LockAction.DELEGATE and UNDELEGATE are defined but unused
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Protocol cannot support vote delegation despite architectural design; users locked for 5 years with no individual exit; dead code increases attack surface and audit complexity
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing delegate(address), undelegate(), quitLock() functions

analysis_questions: Was delegation intentionally removed or accidentally omitted?
Does the dead delegation code path in increaseAmount have correctness issues?
Should the LockedBalance struct be simplified if delegation is not supported?
validation_requirements: Confirm no function changes locked[].delegatee from msg.sender
Verify increaseAmount delegation branch is unreachable
Assess if dead code creates latent bugs if delegation is later added

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Users expecting to delegate their veCANTO voting power to governance representatives or DAOs cannot do so. Users locked for 5 years have no individual early exit mechanism. The entire delegation economics designed into the contract are non-functional.
prerequisites: N/A — missing feature, always applicable

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Users cannot delegate, reducing governance participation efficiency; 5-year lock with no exit creates liquidity risk
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All veCANTO holders
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade or new deployment

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test coverage for delegation or early exit scenarios

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Immediately discoverable by users trying to delegate

threats.title: Gauge-enabled markets may be non-functional due to whitelist key mismatch (market vs gauge address) causing sync_ledger/withdraw to revert
threats.hypothesis_description: When a market is whitelisted with _hasGauge=true, LendingLedger deploys a LiquidityGauge and stores a bi-directional mapping. However, sync_ledger rewrites the effective market key to the LiquidityGauge address, while update_market enforces the whitelist on that rewritten key. Since whiteListLendingMarket only sets lendingMarketWhitelist[_market] (the underlying market), the LiquidityGauge address may not be whitelisted, making update_market revert for all gauge token transfers/mints/burns. This can brick deposits/withdrawals/transfers for gauge-enabled markets.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete, highly realistic integration bug that can halt all gauge-based flows (including withdrawUnderlying) without any special attacker capabilities. It also matches the Phase A logic-analysis 'assumption violation' around whitelist resolution and LiquidityGauge callback coupling.

proof_of_signal.lines: sync_ledger(): lendingMarket = liquidityGauges[msg.sender] when nonzero; then update_market(lendingMarket) requires lendingMarketWhitelist[lendingMarket]
proof_of_signal.pattern: whitelist set on _market, but runtime key becomes LiquidityGauge address

preconditions: Governance called whiteListLendingMarket(_market, true, true) so a LiquidityGauge exists
LiquidityGauge token transfer/mint/burn triggers _afterTokenTransfer which calls LendingLedger.sync_ledger
lendingMarketWhitelist is not set for the LiquidityGauge address (only for the underlying market)

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: LendingLedger.sol sync_ledger + update_market whitelist check; LendingLedger.sol whiteListLendingMarket only whitelists _market; LiquidityGauge.sol _afterTokenTransfer always calls sync_ledger
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: inconsistent market identifier between whitelisting and accounting key
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: any gauge token movement reverts due to whitelist failure, preventing withdrawUnderlying and transfers
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no normalization ensuring update_market checks whitelist against the underlying market key

analysis_questions: For a gauge-enabled market, does sync_ledger pass the gauge address or underlying market to update_market?
Which address does governance actually add as a gauge in GaugeController (market or LiquidityGauge)?
Can governance realistically work around this by whitelisting the LiquidityGauge address as well, and does that introduce other inconsistencies?
validation_requirements: Write an integration test: whitelist market with _hasGauge=true, attempt depositUnderlying/withdrawUnderlying, observe revert source
Confirm mapping direction: liquidityGauges[market]=gauge and liquidityGauges[gauge]=market, and the chosen key in sync_ledger
Check whether lendingMarketTotalBalance and marketInfo keys align across all entry points (sync_ledger, claim, update_market)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Any user tries to depositUnderlying/withdrawUnderlying/transfer gauge tokens; _afterTokenTransfer calls sync_ledger; sync_ledger resolves lendingMarket to the LiquidityGauge address; update_market reverts on 'Market not whitelisted'; user actions become impossible, effectively locking underlying in the gauge for all participants.
prerequisites: A market is onboarded with _hasGauge=true

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Users may be unable to withdraw underlying from LiquidityGauge (hard lock) and/or unable to interact with the market reward accounting
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users of gauge-enabled markets
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Only via governance workaround/migration; no on-chain user escape hatch

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing end-to-end tests for gauge-enabled market flows (whitelist -> depositUnderlying -> withdrawUnderlying).

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: reverts observable immediately on first gauge token transfer

threats.title: LiquidityGauge construction may be DoS'ed by non-standard ERC20 metadata (symbol()) causing market onboarding failure
threats.hypothesis_description: LiquidityGauge constructor calls ERC20(_underlyingToken).symbol() twice to build name/symbol. If the underlying token's symbol() reverts, returns overly large data, or is not ERC20Metadata-compliant, LiquidityGauge deployment will revert, preventing governance from onboarding the market with _hasGauge=true and potentially blocking whitelisting flows that expect gauge deployment to succeed.
threats.investigation_rationale: The constructor makes external calls to an untrusted token contract during deployment. This is a common, practical source of deployment failures and can block protocol expansion or emergency actions (e.g., re-gauging). Even if docs state 'standard ERC20', in production tokens frequently diverge.
deployment_assumptions: Governance whitelists a market with _hasGauge=true where the underlying token has a non-standard or reverting symbol()
threats.config_evidence: LiquidityGauge.sol constructor uses ERC20(_underlyingToken).symbol() twice; LendingLedger.sol whiteListLendingMarket deploys new LiquidityGauge(_market, address(this)) without try/catch.

proof_of_signal.pattern: external token metadata call during construction (no error handling)

preconditions: Governance uses LendingLedger.whiteListLendingMarket with _hasGauge=true
Underlying token does not reliably implement ERC20Metadata.symbol() (reverts / returns invalid ABI / huge string)

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: LiquidityGauge.sol constructor external calls; LendingLedger.sol new LiquidityGauge(...) in whiteListLendingMarket
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: untrusted external call in constructor without try/catch
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: market onboarding can be blocked by token-level revert
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no fallback name/symbol input; no try/catch around metadata calls

analysis_questions: Are the intended underlying assets guaranteed ERC20Metadata-compliant on the target chain?
Could governance accidentally whitelist a contract that is not an ERC20 token (or a proxy in bad state) causing symbol() to revert?
Is it acceptable operationally to require symbol() success, or should the gauge accept explicit name/symbol parameters?
validation_requirements: Test deployment against tokens with (a) reverting symbol(), (b) non-string return, (c) extremely long symbol string
Confirm whether LendingLedger onboarding should proceed without gauge or should revert atomically

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A malicious or simply non-standard market token is proposed/selected; governance calls whiteListLendingMarket(...,_hasGauge=true); LiquidityGauge constructor reverts on symbol(); onboarding fails and cannot be completed without changing the underlying asset or modifying code.
prerequisites: A non-standard token is chosen as underlying

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: No direct theft; prevents adding/using gauges for affected markets; can delay emissions and integrations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users of the affected market; protocol governance operations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Governance can choose different market token or redeploy patched contracts

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for gauge deployment with unusual ERC20Metadata behavior.

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: deployment revert with clear trace to symbol()

threats.title: LendingLedger.setRewards may become uncallable for large epoch ranges (unbounded loop) blocking emission schedule updates
threats.hypothesis_description: setRewards iterates from _fromEpoch to _toEpoch in steps of BLOCK_EPOCH using a for-loop with a <= condition and no upper bound. Large ranges can exceed block gas limits and revert, preventing governance from updating long-horizon reward schedules in a single call.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a direct code-level DoS vector on an admin-critical function, and can be triggered by benign operational attempts (e.g., configuring a year of epochs). While governance-only, it's a practical defect that can block protocol operations.

proof_of_signal.lines: setRewards: for (uint256 i = _fromEpoch; i <= _toEpoch; i += BLOCK_EPOCH) { cantoPerBlock[i] = _amountPerBlock; }
proof_of_signal.pattern: potentially unbounded loop over user-provided range; <= termination

preconditions: _toEpoch - _fromEpoch spans many epochs (large loop count)
Block gas limit is insufficient for the number of iterations

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: unbounded iteration in state-changing function
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: governance cannot update schedules at required horizons; may stall emissions
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no max range; no batching; no events to support partial updates

analysis_questions: What is the maximum safe number of epochs per call under expected gas limits on target chain?
Is there an intended operational pattern to call setRewards in batches?
validation_requirements: Gas benchmark setRewards for increasing range sizes
Confirm whether any UI/ops tooling might attempt large ranges by default

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A normal governance operation attempts to set rewards for a long future window in one transaction; tx reverts due to out-of-gas; emissions remain unset or stale until multiple smaller transactions are executed.
prerequisites: None for existence; only a large parameter range to trigger

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Emissions configuration may lag or fail; users may receive unexpected 0 rewards if schedules not set
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All reward recipients
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Recoverable by batching calls, but failure-prone operationally

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for large setRewards ranges / gas limits.

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: reverts with out-of-gas

threats.title: LendingLedger lacks a withdrawal mechanism for unclaimed native CANTO rewards, potentially locking funds permanently
threats.hypothesis_description: LendingLedger can receive native CANTO via receive() and uses it to pay claims. There is no function for governance to sweep excess/stranded CANTO (e.g., after emission schedule changes or market delisting). This can permanently trap funds in the contract, creating capital inefficiency and complicating migrations.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete code omission affecting fund recoverability. While not an attacker drain, it is a correctness/operational risk for real deployments and upgrades/migrations.

proof_of_signal.lines: receive() external payable {} ; no withdraw/sweep function exists
proof_of_signal.pattern: contract accepts native token but has no privileged recovery path for excess

preconditions: CANTO is sent to LendingLedger in excess of what users claim (or markets are deprecated leaving balances)
No additional contracts exist to pull funds out (none in scope)

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: LendingLedger.sol receive() with no accompanying sweep/withdraw
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: funds can enter but cannot exit except via user claims
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: excess rewards or mistakenly sent CANTO become stuck
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no governance-only recoverNative/recoverERC20 functionality

analysis_questions: Is it intended that all CANTO in LendingLedger must be claimable only by users, with no admin recovery?
How does the protocol handle deprecating markets or migrating to new ledgers?
validation_requirements: Confirm there is no other path (selfdestruct receiver, upgrade) to retrieve funds
Model scenarios where cantoPerBlock decreases and leftover CANTO accumulates

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Not attacker-driven: funds are sent to LendingLedger (funding, donations, mistaken transfers). If emissions are changed or markets removed leaving residual balance, there is no method to recover it, effectively locking treasury funds.
prerequisites: CANTO present in contract beyond what is claimable

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potentially material stranded treasury funds over time
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol treasury/governance; indirectly impacts users via reduced capital efficiency
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None within current code; requires redeploying and abandoning old ledger balance

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for fund recovery / migration scenarios.

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: non-zero contract balance with no extraction path

threats.title: De-Whitelisting Market Permanently Freezes User Funds in LiquidityGauge
threats.hypothesis_description: If governance de-whitelists a market, update_market reverts with 'Market not whitelisted'. LiquidityGauge.withdrawUnderlying calls _burn which triggers _afterTokenTransfer → sync_ledger → update_market, which reverts. All LiquidityGauge functions (withdraw, transfer, transferFrom) trigger _afterTokenTransfer, so ALL user tokens become permanently frozen with no recovery path.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _afterTokenTransfer hook creates a hard dependency on update_market succeeding. De-whitelisting breaks this dependency, creating an irreversible DoS. Users cannot withdraw underlying tokens even though the underlying tokens are physically in the gauge contract.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(lendingMarketWhitelist[_market], 'Market not whitelisted') in update_market; called on every gauge token transfer via _afterTokenTransfer → sync_ledger

preconditions: Market has active LiquidityGauge with user deposits
Governance de-whitelists the market (sets lendingMarketWhitelist to false)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: update_market require(whitelist) → revert propagates through sync_ledger → _afterTokenTransfer → _burn → withdrawUnderlying
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Governance action (even well-intentioned) permanently locks all user funds in the gauge

threats.title: unlockOverride Enables Infinite Voting Power Cycling Attack
threats.hypothesis_description: When unlockOverride is enabled, users can withdraw locked CANTO early. However, GaugeController is NOT notified of the withdrawal — the user's gauge votes remain active until the original 5-year lock expiration. An attacker can: lock CANTO → vote for gauge → withdraw early → transfer CANTO to new address → lock again → vote again, multiplying gauge weight with a fixed capital amount.
threats.investigation_rationale: VotingEscrow.withdraw zeroes the lock and checkpoints VE state, but GaugeController's vote_user_slopes, vote_user_power, and gauge weight points remain unchanged. The stale votes persist and decay based on the original lock_end, not the actual withdrawal. No cross-contract notification exists.

preconditions: unlockOverride is enabled by governance
Attacker has CANTO to lock
Attacker can create multiple addresses (or use one address cyclically)

threats.title: Block Time Drift Causes epochTime to Exceed block.timestamp, Zeroing All Gauge Weights
threats.hypothesis_description: epochTime in update_market is estimated using averageBlockTime and referenceBlockNumber. If actual block production is faster than estimated, epochTime drifts ahead of block.timestamp. GaugeController._get_total only fills points_total up to block.timestamp. When epochTime exceeds block.timestamp by >1 week, gauge_relative_weight_write looks up an unfilled future week where points_total=0, returning weight=0. All market rewards silently become zero.
threats.investigation_rationale: The Canto chain block time may fluctuate. A 3-4% drift over months causes epochTime to lead block.timestamp by a full week, at which point all gauge weights read as 0. This silently destroys all CANTO reward distributions until governance manually corrects the block time parameters.
threats.config_evidence: Deploy.sol sets no block time parameters; defaults in LendingLedger constructor: averageBlockTime=5700, referenceBlockNumber=block.number, referenceBlockTime=block.timestamp

proof_of_signal.pattern: epochTime = referenceBlockTime + ((block.number - referenceBlockNumber) * averageBlockTime) / 1000 — no validation that epochTime <= block.timestamp; GaugeController returns 0 weight for future timestamps

preconditions: Actual average block time is faster than the configured averageBlockTime
Sufficient blocks have passed for drift to exceed one week (~3+ months at 3% drift)
Governance has not called setBlockTimeParameters to correct the drift

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: If actual=5.5s, configured=5.7s → drift=0.2s/block → after 3M blocks (~6 months), drift = 600,000s ≈ 1 week → gauge weights return 0
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Natural block time variation silently zeros all reward distributions
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit; all users lose rewards for affected epochs; recovery requires governance intervention

threats.title: Incomplete Checkpoint Loop Corrupts Global Voting Power After Extended Inactivity
threats.hypothesis_description: The _checkpoint function iterates a maximum of 255 weeks to fill the global point history. Since locks last 260 weeks (5 years), if the contract is inactive for more than 255 weeks, the loop terminates early. The next user action will add their current slope/bias to a stale global point from the past, permanently corrupting the global voting power math.
threats.investigation_rationale: Loop bound is strictly less than the maximum possible time horizon of active state (255 weeks vs 260 weeks), guaranteeing state desynchronization if the gap is hit.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint256 i = 0; i < 255; i++) loop bound is less than LOCKTIME (260 weeks)

preconditions: No state-changing actions occur on VotingEscrow for > 255 weeks

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Loop breaks at 255 weeks, but global point is updated with current block timestamp's user bias
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Adding a present-day user bias to a 5-week-old global point corrupts the linear decay math
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Loop bound should be at least 261 to cover the full LOCKTIME

analysis_questions: Does the math corruption allow users to claim more voting power than they should have?
Can the state be repaired by subsequent checkpoints?

threats.title: Unbounded catch-up loops in GaugeController checkpointing can be exploited to DoS state-changing calls that depend on _get_total/_get_weight

Unprivileged callers can trigger these paths (checkpoint, checkpoint_gauge, gauge_relative_weight_write). If state falls behind (long period without checkpointing or very many gauge types), calls can become too expensive and revert, blocking reward updates that rely on gauge_relative_weight_write.
threats.investigation_rationale: This chain has no public mempool so ordering attacks are excluded, but unprivileged callers can still force expensive execution by calling write functions after long inactivity. LendingLedger.update_market depends on gauge_relative_weight_write, so if that becomes non-callable within gas limits, market reward accounting and sync_ledger paths degrade/brick.

threats.title: Governance weight-change pathways may silently corrupt totals due to unchecked arithmetic growth/overflow in points_total computation
threats.hypothesis_description: GaugeController aggregates weights as `pt += type_sum * type_weight` and stores in uint256 `points_total[t]`. Alternatively, type_weight is used with 1e18 scaling but governance uses raw units incorrectly

proof_of_signal.pattern: unchecked growth in multiplication/addition of weights into global total

Overflow reverts (Solidity 0.8 checked arithmetic)

threats.title: uint128 Overflow DoS Permanently Bricks Market via First-Depositor Attack
threats.hypothesis_description: In update_market, accCantoPerShare and secRewardsPerShare are stored as uint128. The secRewardsPerShare accumulation formula (blockDelta * 1e36 / marketSupply) causes a uint128 overflow revert when marketSupply is very small. An attacker can deposit 1 wei as the first depositor in a new market, and after ~340 blocks the uint128 cast overflows, making update_market permanently revert. Since sync_ledger calls update_market, no user can deposit, withdraw, or claim for that market ever again.
threats.investigation_rationale: secRewardsPerShare += uint128((blockDelta * 1e36) / marketSupply) always accumulates regardless of cantoPerBlock. With marketSupply = 1 wei, a single block adds 1e36 to secRewardsPerShare. After ~340 blocks, the accumulated value exceeds uint128 max (~3.4e38). Solidity 0.8 uint128() cast reverts on overflow.

proof_of_signal.pattern: market.secRewardsPerShare += uint128((blockDelta * 1e36) / marketSupply); with marketSupply = 1, single block yields 1e36, overflow uint128 max ~3.4e38 after ~340 blocks

threats.title: checkpoint_gauge DoS via Slope Underflow in _get_weight After Gauge Removal
threats.hypothesis_description: In _get_weight's catch-up loop, pt.slope -= d_slope is executed without underflow protection when pt.bias > d_bias (Solidity 0.8 checked arithmetic). After remove_gauge_weight modifies changes_weight for a gauge, the remaining slope changes beyond the 263-week cleanup window can cause d_slope > pt.slope during _get_weight iteration, reverting the function. This bricks checkpoint_gauge and gauge_relative_weight_write for the affected gauge, and any update_market call that queries that gauge's weight.
threats.investigation_rationale: _remove_gauge_weight only iterates 263 weeks of future changes_weight. Locks can last up to 260 weeks, but if multiple users voted at different times, slope changes could extend beyond 263 weeks from next_time. Any remaining changes_weight entries cause incorrect slope subtraction. In _get_weight, pt.slope -= d_slope reverts on underflow.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _get_weight: pt.slope -= d_slope without underflow check when pt.bias > d_bias; _remove_gauge_weight only cleans 263 weeks of changes_weight

threats.title: GaugeController unbounded state growth via add_type may permanently DoS checkpointing and weight writes

threats.title: Missing Delegate/Undelegate Functions Create Dead Code Paths and Incomplete Governance Feature
threats.hypothesis_description: VotingEscrow's LockedBalance struct has delegatee and delegated fields, the LockAction enum defines DELEGATE and UNDELEGATE, and increaseAmount contains an else-branch for delegated locks. The delegation code paths in increaseAmount can never execute. The withdraw function's require(locked_.delegatee == msg.sender) check is always true but would block withdrawals if delegation were somehow enabled in the future.
threats.investigation_rationale: Dead code and incomplete features indicate an incomplete implementation that may cause issues if the protocol evolves or if users expect delegation functionality based on the code architecture.

threats.title: Missing quitLock Function Leaves Users Without Early Exit Mechanism for 5-Year Locks
threats.hypothesis_description: The LockAction enum defines QUIT (implying an early exit with penalty, standard in Curve-derived implementations), but no quitLock() function exists. The only escape is the governance-controlled unlockOverride, which is a global all-or-nothing toggle. Users have no individual early exit mechanism, even with a penalty, and governance must risk the voting power amplification bug (OPS-H007) to provide any relief.
threats.investigation_rationale: The absence of an individual early exit mechanism combined with the 5-year lock that resets on every action creates a significant user safety concern. If unlockOverride is the only relief valve, it introduces systemic risk.

threats.title: setRewards Can Retroactively Modify Past Epoch Rates Before Markets Are Updated
threats.hypothesis_description: Governance can call setRewards to change cantoPerBlock for any epoch, including past epochs. If update_market has not been called for a market since before the modified epoch, the next update_market call processes those historical blocks using the NEW cantoPerBlock value. This allows governance to retroactively increase or decrease rewards for specific epochs, with disproportionate impact on markets that haven't been recently updated.
threats.investigation_rationale: setRewards only checks epoch alignment, not whether the epoch is in the past or future. update_market's while loop reads cantoPerBlock[epoch] during catch-up. If cantoPerBlock was changed after the epoch passed but before update_market processed it, the new value is used retroactively.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setRewards writes cantoPerBlock[i] for any epoch range without checking if epoch is past; update_market reads cantoPerBlock[epoch] during catch-up loop
